notes: Birds instinctively mask illness as a survival behaviour. By the time symptoms are visible, the bird is often seriously unwell. Always err on the side of caution and contact an avian-experienced vet quickly.

symptoms.description: Bird sits with feathers fluffed out to trap warm air, often hunched on a perch or on the cage floor. A classic sign of an unwell bird.

possible_causes.brief: Body is trying to keep warm while fighting illness
Cage near a draught or in a cold room
Bird is conserving energy
Trouble passing an egg, can be life-threatening
Lead or zinc poisoning from cage parts, toys, or paint

home_care: Move cage to a warm, draught-free room (around 25-29°C)
Cover three sides of the cage to reduce stress and retain heat
Offer favourite foods to encourage eating
Ensure fresh water is easy to reach
Limit handling - rest is critical
Note any other symptoms (droppings, breathing, posture) to share with the vet

vet_urgency.low: Briefly fluffed when resting or sleeping, otherwise active and eating - keep observing
vet_urgency.medium: Fluffed for several hours, slightly less active - phone an avian vet today
vet_urgency.high: Fluffed continuously, on cage floor, eyes closed, not eating - emergency avian vet immediately

red_flags: Any droppings change (colour, consistency, or none at all)

combinations.concern: Serious systemic illness - urgent avian vet
Respiratory infection or airway disease - EMERGENCY
Possible infection or toxicity - vet within 24h

symptoms.description: Bird repeatedly pulls out, chews, or damages its own feathers, often leaving bald patches on the chest, legs, or under the wings (the head is rarely affected because birds cannot reach it).

possible_causes.brief: Most common in parrots kept alone with few toys
Changes at home, new pets, loud noises, isolation
Itchy skin from mites, lice, or fungal/bacterial infection
Especially for tropical species in centrally heated homes
Breeding season frustration
Seed-only diets lacking vitamins or minerals
Can cause feather damage and poor quality

home_care: Provide foraging toys, puzzle feeders, and rotate toys regularly
Increase out-of-cage time and one-to-one interaction daily
Mist or shower the bird (lukewarm water spray) to improve skin and feather health
Move the cage somewhere quieter if stress seems to be a trigger
Improve diet: add fresh vegetables and a quality pellet if currently seed-only
Keep a diary of when plucking happens to identify triggers

vet_urgency.low: Mild, intermittent plucking, no broken skin - try environmental changes first, see vet if no improvement in 2-3 weeks
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent plucking, visible bald patches, possible skin irritation - avian vet within 1-2 weeks
vet_urgency.high: Self-mutilation drawing blood, open wounds, sudden severe onset - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Bleeding from skin or feather follicles

combinations.concern: Possible underlying systemic illness - vet investigation needed
Infection or severe self-mutilation - avian vet urgently

symptoms.description: Wet or crusty discharge around the nares (nostrils on top of the beak) or eyes. Healthy bird nares are clean and dry.

possible_causes.brief: Including chlamydia (psittacosis) which can spread to humans
Common in seed-only diets, weakens respiratory lining
Air fresheners, cigarette smoke, dusty bedding
Cleaning chemicals, scented candles
Often follows untreated upper respiratory illness

home_care: Remove any airborne irritants (aerosols, candles, smoke, scented plug-ins)
Improve air quality - some owners use HEPA filters near the cage
Keep the bird warm (around 27°C if unwell)
Add vitamin A rich foods: dark leafy greens, sweet potato, carrot
Do not attempt to clean inside the nostrils
Isolate from other birds in case of contagious illness

vet_urgency.low: Brief sneeze with clear droplet, otherwise well - monitor for 24-48 hours
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent clear discharge or repeated sneezing - avian vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Thick, coloured, or crusty discharge, blocked nostrils, or breathing difficulty - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Other birds in household also showing signs

combinations.concern: Likely respiratory infection - vet care needed

symptoms.description: Bird is not eating, or eating noticeably less than normal. Critical to act quickly because small birds can deteriorate within hours of stopping eating.

possible_causes.brief: Reduced appetite is one of the first signs of any illness
Birds can be reluctant to try unfamiliar foods

possible_causes.condition: Stress (new home, new pet, loud noises)
possible_causes.brief: Suppresses appetite
Mechanical difficulty eating
Reproductive emergency
Can suppress appetite quickly

home_care: Offer favourite foods (e.g. millet spray, small piece of fruit)
Warm the bird (around 27-29°C) - cold birds will not eat
Ensure perches and bowls are at comfortable height
Reduce environmental stress (cover sides of cage, dim lights, quiet area)
Weigh daily on a kitchen gram scale - any loss is significant
Do not force-feed - this can cause aspiration

vet_urgency.low: Briefly reduced appetite for under 12 hours, otherwise bright - keep observing closely
vet_urgency.medium: Eating much less than normal for 12-24 hours - phone an avian vet today
vet_urgency.high: Not eating at all for 24+ hours (or 12+ hours in a small bird like a budgie or finch) - emergency avian vet

red_flags: Small bird (budgie, canary, finch) not eating for 12+ hours
Visible weight loss or prominent breastbone (keel)
Lethargy, fluffed feathers, or on cage floor
No droppings or very reduced droppings

combinations.concern: Significant illness or organ disease - urgent vet investigation

symptoms.description: Bird is unusually quiet, sluggish, or unwilling to perch. Sitting on the cage floor is a serious sign in most species (some healthy parrots do play on the floor, but persistent floor-sitting is concerning).

possible_causes.brief: Lethargy is a late-stage sign in birds
PTFE/Teflon fumes, heavy metals, plant toxins
Sprain, fracture, or internal injury
Emergency - bird may strain on cage floor
Cold draughts or low room temperature

home_care: Warm the bird immediately - move to a warm room, cover the cage sides
Remove high perches that the bird might fall from
Place food and water within easy reach on the cage floor
Reduce stress - quiet environment, no handling unless essential
Check for any obvious injury, broken feathers, or bleeding
Prepare to transport to an avian vet

vet_urgency.low: Briefly quiet after a meal or in the evening, otherwise normal - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Notably less active than normal for several hours - phone an avian vet today
vet_urgency.high: On cage floor, eyes closed, fluffed feathers, or unresponsive - EMERGENCY avian vet

red_flags: Not responding to your voice or movement
Any sign of egg-binding in a female (straining, swollen abdomen)

combinations.concern: Possible respiratory failure or toxin exposure - EMERGENCY

symptoms.description: Bird's tail moves up and down with each breath, indicating laboured breathing. Always a serious sign because birds normally breathe almost invisibly.

possible_causes.condition: Respiratory infection (lower airway, lungs, air sacs)
possible_causes.brief: Bacterial, fungal (aspergillosis), or viral
Common in stressed or immunocompromised birds
Parasitic infection of the respiratory tract
Non-stick cookware fumes are rapidly fatal to birds
Pressure on air sacs
More common in older or overweight birds

home_care: Move to a warm, calm environment - do not handle unless absolutely necessary
Remove any potential airborne toxins immediately (turn off non-stick pans, candles, aerosols)
Ensure good ventilation but no draughts
Do not stress the bird - chase or restraint can be fatal in respiratory distress
Contact an avian vet immediately

vet_urgency.low: Mild tail movement only after exertion, settling quickly - still warrants a vet call
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent tail bobbing at rest - urgent avian vet within hours
vet_urgency.high: Tail bobbing with open-mouth breathing, fluffed feathers, or weakness - EMERGENCY

combinations.concern: Respiratory infection progressing - urgent vet care

symptoms.description: Repeated sneezing, audible wheezing, or clicking sounds during breathing. Occasional sneezing can be normal (clearing dust) but persistent or frequent sneezing is a concern.

possible_causes.brief: Bacterial or viral, often from stress or poor husbandry
Weakens respiratory tract lining, especially on seed-only diets
Dust, scented products, cigarette smoke, cooking fumes
Bacterial sinus infection, sometimes from untreated colds
Zoonotic - can spread to humans
Seed husk or debris lodged

home_care: Remove all airborne irritants from the room (aerosols, candles, smoke)
Increase humidity (light misting, humidifier, or shower steam nearby)
Improve diet with vitamin A rich foods if currently on seeds only
Keep the bird warm (around 27°C)
Isolate from other birds if a contagious cause is suspected

vet_urgency.low: Occasional single sneeze, no discharge - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Frequent sneezing or mild wheezing for more than 24 hours - avian vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Persistent wheezing, clicking, laboured breathing, or any discharge - urgent avian vet

symptoms.description: Normally vocal bird becomes quiet, hoarse, or loses its voice. Can indicate respiratory illness affecting the syrinx (voice box) or general unwellness.

possible_causes.brief: Inflammation of the voice box
Quiet behaviour is one of the first signs an unwell bird shows
New home, new pets, changed routine
Fungal lung disease often causes voice changes
Particularly in outdoor-housed birds
Birds are often quieter during heavy moulting

home_care: Look for other signs of illness (fluffed feathers, droppings change, appetite)
Check for moulting feathers - this is normal seasonal behaviour
Reduce stress - quiet environment, regular routine
Improve diet if seed-only
Keep warm if any other unwell signs

vet_urgency.low: Briefly quieter, no other symptoms, possibly moulting - monitor for a week
vet_urgency.medium: Voice change with mild other symptoms - avian vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Hoarse or silent with any breathing difficulty, fluffing, or appetite loss - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Combined with appetite loss or fluffed feathers
Clicking or wheezing sounds when breathing

symptoms.description: Bird droppings have three components: dark faeces, white urates, and clear urine. True diarrhoea means the faecal portion is unformed; polyuria means excess clear water around normal faeces. Both warrant attention.

possible_causes.brief: Fresh fruit and vegetables temporarily increase water content
Especially after antibiotic use or stress
Common in birds with outdoor access
Causes polyuria (excess clear urine)
Lead or zinc poisoning
Lime-green droppings are a classic sign

home_care: Place white kitchen paper under the perch to better assess droppings
Photograph droppings to show the vet
Reduce fresh produce temporarily, stick to pellets and small amount of seed
Ensure plenty of clean water
Keep bird warm and reduce stress
Clean the cage frequently to prevent re-infection

vet_urgency.low: Briefly looser droppings after eating fruit, bird otherwise well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent runny droppings beyond 24 hours - phone avian vet today
vet_urgency.high: Blood, lime-green colour, very dark/black droppings, or no droppings at all - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Pasted vent (droppings stuck to feathers around the vent)

combinations.concern: Serious illness or infection - urgent vet
Chronic illness, possibly organ disease - vet investigation

symptoms.description: The keel (breastbone) becomes prominent and sharp to the touch as the muscle on either side wastes away. Birds hide weight loss well because feathers disguise it. Always weigh on a gram scale to detect early.

possible_causes.brief: Long-term illness drains body condition
Common in seed-based diets and outdoor birds
Progressive weight loss despite eating
More common in older birds, especially budgies
Overgrown or damaged beak prevents proper eating
Psittacine beak and feather disease

home_care: Weigh daily on a digital gram scale to monitor
Offer high-energy foods (millet spray, small amount of seed alongside pellets)
Improve overall diet quality - vegetables, pellets, occasional fruit
Have a faecal sample tested by your vet for parasites
Note appetite, droppings, and activity changes
Keep warm (around 27°C) - underweight birds chill easily

vet_urgency.low: Slight weight fluctuation (less than 5%) - monitor closely
vet_urgency.medium: Steady weight loss over weeks, or 5-10% loss - avian vet within a week
vet_urgency.high: Rapid weight loss, prominent keel, weakness - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Keel feels sharp like a knife edge

combinations.concern: Possible parasites or infection - vet needed

symptoms.description: Female bird is unable to pass an egg through her reproductive tract. A life-threatening emergency, especially in small species (budgies, canaries, finches, cockatiels). Can become fatal within hours.

possible_causes.brief: Soft-shelled eggs stick in the reproductive tract
Inexperienced layers
Excess fat compresses the reproductive tract
Causes muscle weakness and contractions to fail
Physical obstruction
Salpingitis

home_care: This is an emergency - phone an avian vet immediately while preparing to travel
Warm the bird gently (around 29-32°C, this is therapeutic)
Increase humidity (light misting or a humid bathroom)
Do NOT attempt to remove the egg yourself - can cause the egg to break inside and kill the bird
Place on a soft towel in a travel box and keep calm
Note when the bird started straining and any droppings changes

vet_urgency.low: Not applicable - all suspected egg-binding requires urgent vet care
vet_urgency.high: EMERGENCY - any female bird straining, with a swollen abdomen, on the cage floor, or fluffed up after laying activity - immediate avian vet

red_flags: Visibly straining or pumping the tail
Open-mouth breathing (egg pressing on air sacs)
Leg weakness or paralysis (egg pressing on nerves)

symptoms.description: Bird breathes with its beak open. Always a serious sign in birds (unlike dogs, birds do not pant to cool down except in extreme heat). Suggests severe respiratory distress.

possible_causes.brief: Lower airway, lung, or air sac involvement
Non-stick cookware fumes - rapidly fatal
Common in stressed or immunocompromised birds
Inhaled seed husk, food, or liquid
Especially in hot rooms or direct sun
Fluid build-up in the chest

home_care: EMERGENCY - phone an avian vet immediately
Remove any source of fumes or toxins (turn off non-stick pans, open windows)
Move to a calm, well-ventilated area but no cold draughts
Do NOT handle unless absolutely necessary - stress can be fatal
Keep cage on the floor (less far to fall if collapses)
Travel with the cage covered to reduce visual stimulation

vet_urgency.high: EMERGENCY - any open-mouth breathing requires immediate avian vet

red_flags: Pale or blue tongue / inside of mouth

symptoms.description: Healthy droppings have three components: dark green-brown faeces, white urates, and clear urine. Changes in colour can indicate specific illnesses.

possible_causes.brief: Zoonotic disease - urgent diagnosis needed

possible_causes.condition: Yellow or mustard urates - liver disease
possible_causes.brief: Discoloured white portion indicates liver issues

possible_causes.condition: Red or black droppings - blood in faeces
possible_causes.brief: Internal bleeding, ulcer, or heavy metal toxicity

possible_causes.condition: Red urine - heavy metal poisoning (lead, zinc)
possible_causes.brief: Wine-coloured urine portion is classic
Harmless if recent diet matches

possible_causes.condition: Very dark droppings - dehydration or upper GI bleeding
possible_causes.brief: Black tarry droppings suggest digested blood

home_care: Photograph the droppings (with paper underneath) for the vet
Note recent diet - some foods cause harmless colour changes
Check the cage for any chewed metal items or paint
Isolate from other birds in case of contagious cause
Keep the bird warm and reduce handling
Bring a fresh dropping sample to the vet appointment

vet_urgency.low: Dietary colour change matching recent food, bird otherwise well - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Sustained colour change beyond 24 hours with no dietary cause - avian vet within a few days
vet_urgency.high: Lime-green, red, black, or wine-coloured urine - urgent avian vet

red_flags: Combined with lethargy or appetite loss
Household has other ill birds or unwell humans (psittacosis is zoonotic)
